title: TBM 392: When (And How) Tools Matter
questions: When do tools actually improve adoption of a behavior in an organization?
How do you choose the right tool intervention based on what’s blocking the behavior?
What should you do when a behavior is aspirational, inconsistent, friction-filled, or actively suppressed?
How should AI be used differently across capability, opportunity, and motivation gaps?
search_terms: political risk social norms behavior change
summary: This post explains how to decide whether tools will help (and how) by treating organizational behaviors as being in different states on a realization spectrum. It uses the COM-B model to map specific blockers—capability, opportunity, and motivation—to the kinds of tool interventions that work. It’s aimed at practitioners designing change, workflows, or AI support who need a state-dependent way to choose interventions rather than assuming tools are universally helpful.

title: TBM 358: The Genius of SVPG
questions: How do you pitch organizational change so executives can absorb it without losing face?
Why do internal change efforts get discounted even when the content is correct?
What structural constraints prevent product operating model adoption?
How do you run transformation while keeping the organization operating day to day?
search_terms: no one gets fired for recommending Transformed
continuous improvement in high-performing tech teams
summary: The post argues that SVPG and Transformed are unusually effective at driving product and organizational change because they use executive-friendly narrative framing and timing. It’s aimed at internal change agents and product leaders who need practical guidance on how to get buy-in, run transformation in parallel with day-to-day work, and address structural constraints—not just skills. It also broadens the change lens by contrasting stewardship-style transformation with slower, messier approaches like sensemaking and learning.

title: TBM 357: Perspectives on Change and Improvement
questions: What different views on change and improvement do I hold, and what strengths and risks come with each?
What made my current stance feel justified at the time, and where might I be rationalizing?
Where do I dismiss promising ideas too quickly because of context or past failures?
How do I balance psychological safety and cohesion with the need for hard decisions and conflict?
search_terms: theory to practice gap in improvement
summary: The post offers a reflective framework for understanding how individuals think about change and improvement, emphasizing that perspectives are best viewed as a continuum with predictable strengths and blind spots. It’s aimed at leaders, practitioners, and team members who want to diagnose their own rationalizations, recognize contextual constraints, and avoid common traps when trying to improve organizations. Readers are prompted to map their current and past stances to the risks of idealization, oversimplification, theory-only thinking, or avoiding necessary tension.

title: TBM 345: Back On Track
questions: How do I tell whether I need a transformation specialist versus a product leader to drive change?
What’s the best way to balance showing great outcomes with navigating a protracted change period?
How can I increase execution speed by giving teams the strategic context behind decisions?
How do I design behavior changes so people feel agency and aren’t just being experimented on?
search_terms: context and expiration date change management
people hate being changed agency experimentation
bias assuming internal people lack skills
summary: This post offers three leadership lessons for driving organizational change: transformation requires distinct skills from product leadership, and leaders must balance showing what good looks like with navigating long periods of influence without authority. It also argues that people tolerate experimentation better when leaders provide strategic context plus an expiration date, and it provides a practical prompt leaders can use to elicit the decision trade-offs behind execution. The guidance is aimed at product and organizational leaders who need to improve change outcomes and decision quality during disruption.

title: TBM 320: From Fluffy Concepts to Concrete Outcomes & Behaviors
questions: What observable behaviors would we see more or less of if we were truly more empowered or more data-driven?
Why do people advocate for or resist practices they haven’t tried first-hand?
How do we move from desired outcomes to specific behaviors and measurable conditions for change?
What interventions are most likely to enable behavior change given capability, opportunity, and motivation constraints?
search_terms: Talk to the Elephant behavior change
summary: This post is for product leaders and product teams who are frustrated that aspirational concepts like empowerment and being data-driven don’t translate into day-to-day practice. It argues for replacing vague goals with observable behaviors, then using COM-B and behavior change techniques to diagnose barriers and choose interventions. The guidance is especially relevant when teams need to improve how they review metrics and make decisions.

title: TBM 288: Changing Your Swing
questions: How should we approach organizational change without creating chaos and blame?
What does it mean to “rebuild the swing” in a team or company context?
How do we support teams when they feel incompetent during a transition?
Should we abandon multi-year strategy and annual planning during major change?
summary: The post uses Tiger Woods’ repeated swing rebuilds as a metaphor for how organizations should handle change. It argues that companies often respond with rushed planning, structural overhauls, and blame, whereas effective change comes from slowing down, diagnosing the current state, and rebuilding habits through intentional practice, reflection, and fast feedback. It’s aimed at leaders and teams navigating transformation who want a calmer, learning-oriented approach rather than faux certainty.

title: TBM 284: Why Aren't We Talking About Continuous Improvement?
questions: How do you interpret the phrase continuous improvement?
What are you seeing in your organization or industry regarding continuous improvement?
Why do layoffs and transformation narratives in tech rarely include employee participation in fixing problems?
What leadership conditions enable teams to co-design change rather than have it imposed?
search_terms: lean agile culture respect for people
summary: The post argues that continuous improvement (kaizen) is largely missing from contemporary tech conversations about transformation and layoffs, even when teams are struggling. It connects this gap to a lack of respect for people, employee engagement, and co-designed change, suggesting entrenched power structures and organizational inertia. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners in lean/agile who want to understand why kaizen discourse fades and how empowered leadership can restart a continuous improvement flywheel.

title: TBM 263: "I Aspire to Regret How We Used to Work"
questions: Why does team improvement sometimes look like it “just happened” without changing team composition?
How can we avoid the limiting belief that if improvement is easy, something must be wrong?
What light-touch interventions help teams explore and adapt without forcing big changes?
How can we position teams to surf shifting product, customer, and employee expectations?
search_terms: team metrics batting average throughput retention
what got us here will not get us there
summary: The post argues that teams often improve through shifting enabling conditions rather than a single new expertise or leader, and that our “improvement dissonance” leads us to misread what’s happening. It’s aimed at product and team leaders who want to understand why progress in product development is non-linear and influenced by luck, timing, and context. The piece emphasizes continuous learning, experimentation, and creating environments where teams can adapt lightly and effectively.

title: TBM 226: Stuck In The Valley of "Doing OK"
questions: Why do companies drift from “doing OK” into obscurity instead of improving?
How can leaders and teams create meaningful change when the situation is not obviously broken?
What should you do when improvement efforts threaten core strengths and trigger risk aversion?
How do you disrupt a system safely without triggering a threat response?
search_terms: seeding new behaviors to create new attractors
summary: This post explains why organizations can become stuck in a “doing OK” plateau that slowly drifts toward obscurity, using dynamic-systems ideas like basins of attraction. It’s aimed at leaders and team members trying to drive change in environments where problems are on the radar but decisive action feels risky or unnecessary. The author argues for safe disruption—seeding behaviors, increasing connectivity, adjusting incentives, and timing localized actions—rather than complacency accusations or superficial reorgs.

title: TBM 207: Organic, Planned, and Manager-Drive Change
questions: How does change actually happen in my company—organic, planned, or manager-driven?
What are the risks and failure modes of each change model in my context?
How can we enable organic change across departments at scale without creating silos or excessive cognitive load?
What should boundary-spanning roles (e.g., UX research, product management) do when different parts of the org use different change models?
search_terms: organic vs planned vs manager-driven change
summary: The post proposes a practical way to diagnose how organizational change really happens by categorizing it into organic/incremental, planned/top-down, and manager-driven decentralized change. It explains the typical risks of each model and how organizational structure and incentives determine which model dominates across departments. It is aimed at product and boundary-spanning leaders who need to navigate mixed change cultures without trying to force a one-size-fits-all transformation.

title: TBM 11/52: Scaffolding & Skepticism
questions: How can we collect dependency data without turning it into a permanent bureaucratic burden?
What makes people skeptical about new corporate initiatives, and how do we address that skepticism credibly?
How do we ensure a temporary process is removed rather than becoming the new normal?
When someone says teams need training wheels, how can we tell whether it’s for help or for selling a framework?
summary: This post argues that temporary “scaffolds” like dependency lists and visualization systems can be necessary, but only if leaders treat them as time-boxed experiments with a clear plan to remove them. It’s aimed at managers and senior leaders who want to reduce costly inter-team dependencies while avoiding the trap of institutionalizing bureaucracy. It also reframes skepticism as rational concern about follow-through and incentives, offering guidance on how to earn trust through accountability, expiration dates, and feedback loops.

title: TBM 10/52: Subtractive and Additive Change
questions: How do I distinguish additive vs subtractive change in practice?
Why do organizations default to adding new processes instead of removing old ones?
What are the risks of many small additive changes (paper cuts) or many local subtractive changes (workarounds)?
What should we remove to make people's lives instantly easier, and how can leadership facilitate that win?
summary: The post argues that organizational change should be viewed as a polarity between subtractive and additive change, not as inherently good or bad. It helps leaders and operators recognize why companies default to adding work and processes, and how small changes can create global drag or shift burdens elsewhere. It is aimed at people leading improvement efforts who want a practical way to identify and execute subtractive wins that make work instantly easier.

title: TBM 37/52: (Dis)incentives
questions: Why do incentive programs backfire or produce unintended outcomes?
What disincentives are preventing collaboration or experimentation in my team?
How can we shift from reward-based management to environment and support-based enablement?
What should we change first: the reward structure or the operational constraints?
search_terms: make it easier to do good things
summary: The post argues that organizational incentive schemes often fail because they ignore disincentives and downstream effects, and it encourages leaders to focus on removing friction and improving support structures. It’s aimed at managers and leaders who want to drive behaviors like collaboration and experimentation without unintended consequences. It also frames this as a practical exercise: list the disincentives blocking the behavior you want to incentivize.

title: TBM 27/52: When Is Change More Likely?
questions: What actually predicts whether an organizational change effort will lead to lasting behavior change?
How can leaders tell the difference between change theater and structural change?
Which system leverage points should we target to shift incentives, constraints, and information flows?
What kinds of interventions have the best odds of improving outcomes in corporate settings?
search_terms: where to intervene in a system for change
power dynamics and collaboration patterns in change
summary: The post argues that lasting change in organizations usually requires structural shifts—such as changes to priorities, team structures, incentives/punishments, dependencies, and constraints—rather than surface-level process updates and “talk.” It is aimed at leaders and managers who want to increase the odds that transformation efforts produce real behavioral change, using systems thinking and leverage points as a guide.

title: Making Things Better (With Enabling Constraints and POPCORN)
questions: How can we design constraints that align people and improve outcomes instead of forcing premature convergence or workarounds?
What makes an enabling constraint “good,” and how do we tell when it’s becoming disabling?
How do we run improvement experiments safely when the system is messy and full of dependencies?
How should we scale a local practice—experiment first, then expand, and finally run a full program?
summary: The post explains how to improve organizational outcomes by designing “enabling constraints” that align team behavior through context-sensitive rules, while avoiding constraints that cause premature convergence or disable trust. It provides guidance for running enabling constraints as time-bound, safe-to-fail experiments and introduces POPCORN Flow as a lightweight framework for planning, committing, reviewing, and iterating improvement bets. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to make change stick without overwhelming people, and who need a path from local experiments to scaled programs.

title: TBM 14b/52: Discontinuous Improvement
questions: Why does improvement often get worse before it gets better?
What makes 'continuous improvement' fail in real organizations?
How do you prepare teams for the temporary disruption of change?
How can teams visualize work when it’s painful and not everyone agrees on what work is?
search_terms: visualize the work pain and costs
summary: The post challenges the assumption that improvement is truly continuous, arguing that real progress is often discontinuous and may temporarily worsen due to visibility costs, coordination friction, and organizational constraints. It uses the renovation “scaffolding” analogy to explain why change agents should expect temporary disruption and plan to remove temporary processes once alignment improves. The piece is aimed at leaders and teams practicing improvement who need a more realistic mindset for sustaining change.

title: TBM 7/52: Making Room For New Things
questions: How do we make room for new, high-value activities without burning out or failing to build habits?
What reactive and low-value “familiar” work is draining our time and energy on autopilot?
Are the activities we consider high value still high value, or have they drifted into low-value behavior?
Are we stuck in a cycle of fighting fires that prevents proactive habit-building?
search_terms: reactive work heroics vs proactive habits
initiative reviews and starting initiatives together
summary: The post argues that adopting new team practices is an intentional behavior change that feels uncomfortable at first and demands practice, motivation, and a supportive environment. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who are trying to start initiatives together or run initiative reviews, but are trapped by reactive firefighting and familiar low-value work. The core guidance is to remove or reduce other activities to create energy and attention for high-value new work, and to periodically audit what has drifted into autopilot.

title: TBM 46b/52: 7 Lessons for Sustainable Change Agency
questions: Why does nothing seem to happen after years of change work, and when will it finally pay off?
If my organization seems resistant, how can I understand how change is actually happening right now?
How do I design experiments that are safe, small, and likely to produce useful learning?
How can I avoid burnout and decide whether to stay in a role while driving change?
search_terms: treat change agency as a product
summary: This post offers seven practical lessons for people trying to nudge organizations in new directions, especially during high-stress periods like the pandemic. It focuses on sustaining change agents over time by understanding nonlinear timing, designing safe-to-fail experiments, and working with real organizational dynamics rather than assumed resistance. It’s aimed at product-minded change agents who want leverage without burning out.

title: TBM 6/52: Discontinuous Improvement (and Stored Energy)
questions: Why does my improvement work feel like it goes nowhere for months?
How can we avoid burnout when feedback is delayed or progress reverses?
What causes change to “click” after a long period of resistance?
How do we practice improvement sustainably when results are not linear?
summary: The post argues that organizational improvement is often discontinuous: progress may appear absent until a catalyst triggers adoption. It is aimed at people doing long-running change work who feel demoralized by delayed feedback, offering a “stored energy” mindset and guidance on practicing sustainably to avoid burnout.

title: TMB 40/53: 10 Tips for Sustainable Change Agency
questions: How do I lead change when people support me privately but won’t take risks publicly?
What should I do when my “easy fix” approach undermines others’ autonomy?
How can I tell whether a conversation is stalled and unlikely to move forward?
When should I wait for a catalyst versus keep pushing the same approach?
search_terms: psychological safety public vs private support
walk away power for change leaders
summary: The post offers practical, experience-based tips for people trying to drive organizational change sustainably. It is aimed at change agents and leaders who need to manage influence, timing, and human dynamics (autonomy, risk boundaries, stalled conversations, and self-awareness) to keep progress moving over the long term.

title: TBM 34/53: Better Experiments
questions: How can we design internally-focused experiments that better fit our context?
What tradeoffs should we consider when choosing between local vs global, short vs long, and flexible vs rigid experiments?
How do we avoid getting stuck in one experiment style (e.g., always small pilots or always big-bang programs)?
How can we select an experiment that balances blast radius, dependencies, feedback speed, and participant motivation?
summary: The post offers a facilitation activity for teams to design internally-focused experiments, especially during pandemic-era challenges like burnout and remote-work adaptation. It helps teams build a shared vocabulary by brainstorming “extremes” across multiple experimentation dimensions (scope, duration, flexibility, motivation, dependencies, feedback loops, and second/third-order effects) and then selecting a candidate via voting. It’s aimed at team leads and practitioners who want a practical way to improve experiment quality and fit to context.

title: TBM 31/53: Scaling Change Experiments
questions: What are the stages of scaling change experiments in an organization?
How do you know when a local practice is ready to scale?
What signals indicate scaling is working or failing?
How can leaders avoid top-down rollouts without local proof points and advocates?
search_terms: top-down change without local proof points
signals to know when to scale
summary: The post outlines a staged approach to organizational change: start with safe, local experiments, then run additional experiments to test whether scaling is possible and desirable, and only then launch a full program with education and support. It is aimed at leaders and managers who want to scale successful practices without assuming they will work everywhere, and who need practical guidance on avoiding common failure modes like skipping steps or running top-down rollouts too early.

title: TBM 18/53: Blank Slates, Seedlings, Freight Trains, and Gardening
questions: Where do we feel the urge to start over, and what drives it?
What walls divide us but are necessary, and which walls should be strengthened or removed?
What efforts are we protecting like seedlings, and do they get a chance to grow?
What are our freight trains, and where do they run without us questioning their value?
search_terms: blank slate start over knowing what we know now
summary: The post offers a practical team exercise that uses everyday metaphors (blank slates, walls, seedlings, freight trains, gardening) to surface tensions and alternative explanations during discussion. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want to guide conversations about change, constraints, and where teams should invest, prune, or question existing momentum.

title: TBM 3/53: Experiment With Not On
questions: How do we run process experiments without making people feel like they’re being experimented on?
What should a good experiment brief include (why, progress signals, risks, and decision points)?
How can leaders improve buy-in for new processes or metrics?
When should we pivot or proceed after a trial period?
summary: The post argues that process improvements work better when leaders treat changes as experiments with a clear why and invite affected teams to act as co-experimenters. It provides a practical framing for experiments—defining progress signals, risks to watch for, accountability, and a time-boxed decision to pivot or proceed. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to implement new processes or metrics without eroding trust.
